meta.name: Roots Academy Tarbiyyah Audit Framework
meta.description: Three-framework audit for assessing Islamic formation in schools

1.description: Element not observed at all during the visit

2.description: Briefly or unintentionally present; occurs at most once; minimal or no formation impact

3.description: Clearly present with some intentionality; inconsistent across the lesson or across student groups

4.description: Consistent and intentional throughout the observation; clear, observable impact on student thinking or behaviour

5.description: Deeply embedded; students actively demonstrate the impact without prompting; culture holds without the teacher driving it

NA.description: Evidence genuinely insufficient from this session type; do NOT use as low score proxy

F1.name: Teacher in the Classroom
F1.centralQuestion: When a student walks out of this classroom, have they encountered a teacher who models and embodies the values they are being asked to develop, or simply one who delivers content?

1A.name: Teacher Character & Presence
1A.description: Does the teacher embody Islamic character in the way they show up, communicate, and relate to students?

1A1.name: Adab with Students
1A1.focus: Quality of relational conduct, does it reflect Islamic values of respect, warmth, and dignity in all interactions, including under pressure?

1: Interrupts students mid-sentence or uses sarcastic/dismissive language more than once
Visible frustration: raised voice, eye-rolls, or sighing during the lesson
Students appear guarded or compliant, no warmth observable in any interaction
2: Polite in routine exchanges but lapses into impatience at least once during disruption
Warmth shown selectively, some students receive it, others visibly do not
Uses student names mainly in corrective, not positive, contexts
3: Respectful tone in most interactions; rarely raises voice
Warmth present but uneven, stronger one-on-one than during whole-class moments
No Islamic framing of the teacher-student relationship (no husn al-zan, brother/sister language)
4: Consistent calm and warmth throughout, including during disruptions
Corrections delivered quietly; names used positively not just correctively
Islamic framing present at least once (e.g. husn al-zan, ukhuwwah reference)
5: Students themselves demonstrate the adab they have absorbed, peers don't laugh when someone is corrected
Culture holds without enforcement: room tone doesn't change if teacher steps out briefly
Auditor cannot identify a single interaction that compromised student dignity

1A2.name: Self-Regulation Under Pressure
1A2.focus: Does the teacher model sabr when disrupted or challenged, or does their emotional state negatively shape the room?

1: Loses composure more than once, raises voice significantly, reacts with sarcasm or visible anger
Shuts down student questions dismissively at least once
Room becomes visibly tense when teacher is stressed
2: Loses composure once, a sharp response or raised voice
Recovers quickly but does not acknowledge or repair the moment
Sabr is not named or modelled for students
3: Generally calm but visible strain during disruption, clipped responses, shortened patience
No outburst, but composure is effortful rather than natural
Self-regulation not named or modelled for students
4: Encounters at least one genuine disruption and responds with composure, voice level and language respectful
Room is not penalised for one student's behaviour
If lesson is uneventful, ask students what the teacher does when someone misbehaves
5: Students self-regulate their own responses in moments of frustration rather than reacting immediately
If asked, students connect their teacher's composure to Islamic concepts (sabr, hilm)
Teacher's emotional regulation is a visible formation outcome, not just classroom management

1A3.name: Building Trust and Rapport
1A3.focus: Does the teacher genuinely know their students, and do students experience this as safety rather than surveillance?

1: Does not appear to know all students' names
Interactions are purely instructional, no personal connection observable
Students seem compliant rather than engaged; no sense they feel known or cared for
2: Knows some names; makes surface-level attempts to connect
One-on-one warmth not visible
Students don't seek out or initiate interaction beyond answering questions
3: Knows names and shows genuine interest but rapport is primarily whole-class
Warmth present but students don't feel individually known
A student carrying something difficult would not necessarily see this teacher as approachable
4: References individual students meaningfully, acknowledges their context, adapts based on knowing that student
Students initiate interaction beyond answering questions
At least one interaction shows prior knowledge of a student's situation or character
5: Students actively seek out this teacher, observed approaching before or after the lesson without prompting
When asked who they'd go to with a personal or faith concern, this teacher is named spontaneously
Students describe feeling genuinely known, not managed

1A4.name: Modelling Islamic Character
1A4.focus: Is the teacher's visible conduct itself a lesson in Islamic values, or is Islamic character in words but not in manner?

1: No visible connection between teacher's conduct and Islamic values
Manner in class is indistinguishable from a secular classroom
Islamic character may be in lesson content but is not embodied in behaviour
2: Occasionally uses Islamic language (be patient, have good manners) but own behaviour doesn't consistently reflect it
The gap between what is said and what is modelled is observable by the auditor
Islamic character referenced but not lived in the same lesson
3: Conduct generally consistent with Islamic values, patient, fair, respectful, but the Islamic dimension is implicit
A student experiences a decent teacher but would not specifically connect the conduct to Islamic character
Teacher does not name or frame their own behaviour in Islamic terms
4: At least once, teacher's conduct is itself a visible lesson, acknowledges a mistake naming it as tawadu, shows genuine rahma in student struggle, or names a composed response as sabr
Islamic character is lived and named, not just assumed
Students can see Islamic values in action in this classroom
5: Students can articulate Islamic character they observe in their teacher unprompted, using specific vocabulary (sabr, adl, rahma, tawadu)
The teacher's character is a point of reference in student conversation
The modelling has produced observable outcomes in how students speak about and relate to each other

1A5.name: Dignity in Correction
1A5.focus: When a student misbehaves or makes an error, does the teacher's response protect student dignity, or diminish it?

1: Corrections are public, harsh, or shaming, sarcasm, calling out errors in front of the class
At least one corrective interaction leaves a student visibly diminished or embarrassed
No effort to protect student dignity in any corrective moment observed
2: Corrections mostly public, delivered to the room rather than the individual
No active shaming but no active protection of dignity either
Student reaction (lowered eyes, slumped posture) suggests they feel exposed
3: Corrections are generally fair but happen publicly by default
Tone is neutral rather than warm; correction communicates the rule but not care for the student
No Islamic framing of correction (dignity, growth, reparation)
4: Default for serious corrections is private, quiet word alongside the desk or a brief step outside
Minor redirections happen without drawing class attention
At least once, a correction functions as an invitation rather than a judgment
5: Students who are corrected look heard, not humiliated
If a correction happens in view of others, peers respond with gravity not laughter, dignity is peer-to-peer
After correction, the student re-enters the lesson intact, engagement does not deteriorate

1B.name: Islamic Integration in Lesson Delivery
1B.description: Does the teacher connect subject matter to an Islamic worldview, or does the lesson exist in a secular vacuum?

1B1.name: Tawheed Connection
1B1.focus: Does the teacher connect subject matter to Allah, creation, or an Islamic worldview? Applies to ALL subjects, not only Islamic Studies.

1: No connection to Allah, Islamic values, or Islamic understanding of the subject
Intellectual framework is entirely secular, whether in Islamic Studies or Maths
No tawheedic lens applied at any point during the lesson
2: A tokenistic mention of Islam, an opening Bismillah without explanation or 'this is part of Allah's creation' tacked on
Not meaningfully connected to lesson content, could be removed without educational loss
Students do not engage with the Islamic mention
3: One or two genuine attempts to connect the lesson to an Islamic perspective
Intentional but not developed or returned to across the lesson
The tawheedic lens is an addition to the lesson, not an integration into it
4: At least one clear, substantive tawheedic connection woven into content, not bolted on at start or end
Teacher draws a natural link between subject matter and Allah's wisdom or command
Students respond to the connection, it is not just stated and moved on from
5: Students articulate the tawheedic dimension of the subject without being prompted
When asked why this subject matters as a Muslim, students answer substantively with their own reasoning
The framework has become students' own way of thinking, not a layer the teacher adds

1B2.name: Gratitude and Basmala Culture
1B2.focus: Is Islamic intention and gratitude meaningful, or purely habitual? Do students understand why, or are they going through the motions?

1: Lesson begins with no Islamic framing, no Bismillah, du'a, or niyyah
Opened and closed as a secular lesson would be
No basmala culture of any kind observable
2: Teacher opens with Bismillah but it is rushed or done while still arranging materials
Students follow mechanically, no Hamdala or closing
Habit without presence: formulaic, not meaningful
3: Opens with Bismillah with some intentionality, pauses, settles the class first
Niyyah not explained or connected to the lesson, students follow but don't appear to understand why
Closing Hamdala may be absent or an afterthought
4: Opening is intentional and unhurried, teacher briefly names the niyyah
Students understand why they begin this way, if asked, they can explain in their own words
Hamdala at the close is present and not perfunctory
5: Students initiate or lead the basmala culture, a student begins the du'a without being asked
Students remind each other if it is missed
Practice is owned by the class, not imposed by the teacher

1B3.name: Use of Prophetic Examples
1B3.focus: Is the Seerah a living resource in this teacher's delivery, or is the Prophet (SAW) only referenced in Islamic Studies lessons?

1: No reference to the Prophet (SAW), Sahabah, or Islamic history during the observation
Even in Islamic Studies, lessons rely on abstract principles without prophetic grounding
The Seerah does not exist as a formation resource in this classroom
2: Prophet (SAW) mentioned briefly but not integrated, a passing reference not unpacked
Connection to lesson content not made clear
Students don't engage with it, could be cut without changing the lesson
3: A prophetic example is used to illustrate a point, connection to lesson content is clear
Remains illustrative: the story supports the point rather than forming the heart of it
Students receive it passively; they don't engage with or discuss it
4: At least one prophetic example, ayah, or Seerah narrative genuinely illuminates the lesson theme
Teacher unpacks it with skill, invites student reflection or connects it to students' own lives
The Seerah functions as a living reference, not a historical footnote
5: Students reference prophetic examples independently during the lesson, without being prompted
When asked which prophetic examples they remember, students answer with depth and personal connection
Students are carriers of the Seerah, not just recipients of it

1B4.name: Questions That Develop Thinking About Deen
1B4.focus: Does the teacher ask questions that invite students to connect knowledge to their identity as Muslims, or are questions used only to check comprehension?

1: Questions are exclusively closed, comprehension checks, factual recall, or procedural
No question invites students to reflect, apply to life, or connect to their identity as a Muslim
Teaching is information delivery; students are passive recipients
2: Occasionally asks a broader question but without Islamic framing or formation intent
Question fades quickly, lesson moves on regardless of student response
No genuine expectation that the question will produce reflection
3: At least one question invites reflection beyond comprehension, a 'why does this matter?' or 'how would a Muslim approach this?'
Not well-designed or followed through, teacher accepts first response and moves on
Students are not consistently encouraged to ask their own deeper questions
4: Uses 2-3 questions clearly designed to connect knowledge to Islamic identity
Students engage genuinely, questions are taken seriously and discussed, not skimmed
Teacher responds to student thinking with real curiosity
5: Students generate their own questions connecting lesson content to their deen without being prompted
Classroom is a space of genuine Islamic inquiry, students push back and develop each other's thinking
Teacher facilitates rather than leads; the inquiry is student-driven

1B5.name: Integration with 5D Thinking Framework
1B5.focus: Does the teacher apply the school's 5D Thinking approach? Applies to ALL subject teachers, for Islamic Studies, explicit 5D connection; for general subjects, tawheed-integrated delivery.

1: No evidence of 5D Thinking or tawheed-integrated delivery
Teacher appears unaware of the framework, or aware but entirely ignoring it
Lesson is delivered with no Islamic epistemological framing whatsoever
2: Aware of 5D framework and mentions it but doesn't meaningfully apply it
Any integration attempt feels forced or tokenistic and is not returned to
Teacher cannot explain how 5D Thinking applies to their subject
3: Makes a genuine attempt to apply the 5D framework, connects the subject to tawheed in one part of the lesson
Integration works in one part and disappears in another
Feels like something still being learned rather than embedded in planning
4: 5D framework is a visible, natural part of how the teacher has planned and delivers the lesson
Integration feels organic, not performed, students can see the Islamic dimension of what they studied
If asked after the lesson, students can articulate the Islamic dimension of their subject
5: Students use 5D language or a tawheedic lens independently during the lesson
When asked what their faith has to do with this subject, students answer substantively without hesitation
The framework has become students' own intellectual tool, not a layer the teacher adds

1C.name: Classroom Environment & Culture
1C.description: Does the physical and emotional environment of the classroom reflect Islamic values?

1C1.name: Physical Environment
1C1.focus: Does the classroom communicate an active Islamic identity connected to formation? Assess what this teacher has built in their own room, not shared corridors (Framework 2).

1: Classroom displays are entirely academic or generic, no Islamic content whatsoever
A visitor would not know this is an Islamic school from this room alone
Islamic identity is entirely absent from the physical environment
2: A token Islamic element present, a Quranic ayah or calligraphy poster, but incidental
Unrelated to current learning, unchanged across the year, not engaged with by teacher or students
It is decoration, not formation
3: Several Islamic elements visible, du'a, hadith, character reminders, some connected to the subject
Static: unchanged for months, students no longer notice them
Teacher does not reference them in lessons
4: Displays are connected to current learning themes, recently updated or created with student input
Teacher references wall displays in lessons
A visitor can read this room and understand something about what this class is working on spiritually and academically
5: Students have contributed to and take visible ownership of the Islamic environment
Displays include student work reflecting genuine formation, their own reflections or creations alongside Islamic texts
Students notice and reference the walls, the room reflects their formation journey

1C2.name: Culture of Respect and Cooperation
1C2.focus: Do students treat each other with Islamic fraternity? Assess what this teacher has cultivated in their room, not school-wide peer culture (Framework 2).

1: Student interactions reflect no Islamic framing, dismissiveness, laughing at wrong answers, visible social exclusion
Teacher does not address unkind peer dynamics or connect them to Islamic values
Islamic brotherhood/sisterhood is entirely absent from how students relate
2: Teacher promotes general respect but without Islamic framing
Students comply when reminded but revert quickly
Ukhuwwah, brotherhood/sisterhood rooted in shared faith, is entirely absent
3: Teacher occasionally references Islamic values in peer relations, 'we're brothers and sisters', 'give husn al-zan'
Students generally cooperate but Islamic framing is occasional, not embedded
Cooperation is compliance-driven rather than values-driven
4: Teacher has cultivated a culture where Islamic brotherhood/sisterhood is an operative value, not just a phrase
When a student struggles, others don't laugh, not from fear of the teacher but because the culture doesn't permit it
Teacher explicitly frames cooperation in Islamic terms at least once during the observation
5: Students demonstrate ukhuwwah spontaneously, helping without being asked, including quieter students, defending peers
Students can articulate why they treat each other this way and connect it to shared Muslim identity
Culture holds in the teacher's absence

1C3.name: Accountability Without Humiliation
1C3.focus: Does accountability reflect the Islamic balance of justice and rahma? Focus on this teacher's practice, not the school-wide behaviour system (Framework 2).

1: Consequences are public, harsh, or shaming, sarcasm, calling out behaviour loudly, disproportionate consequences
At least one incident observed that leaves a student publicly diminished
No effort to protect student dignity in any corrective moment
2: Consequences are inconsistent, sometimes private and proportionate, sometimes public and reactive
Teacher's approach shaped by their own frustration level rather than a consistent principle
Students appear uncertain about what to expect
3: Consequences generally fair and not punitive but applied procedurally
Rule enforced, consequence given, without connection to growth, restoration, or Islamic concepts of tawbah
Accountability is about compliance, not formation
4: Consequences are clearly related to the behaviour, reasoned, and private where appropriate
At least once, teacher frames accountability beyond the rule, naming impact on others, inviting reflection, connecting to a shared value
An Islamic concept of accountability is referenced or the approach is fully consistent with it
5: Students articulate the reason for accountability in Islamic terms without being prompted
When a student is held accountable in view of others, peers respond with gravity rather than entertainment
Students demonstrate self-accountability, correcting their own behaviour before the teacher intervenes

1C4.name: Student Voice Is Heard
1C4.focus: Are students genuinely invited to think, question, and contribute, or is the lesson a performance by the teacher that students passively receive?

1: Teacher dominates entirely, extended talk with minimal student contribution beyond yes/no responses
When students attempt to contribute beyond expected responses, they are redirected or cut off
Students appear to have learned that their thinking is not what is being asked for
2: Students invited to respond to questions but the invitation is closed, right answer sought, divergent responses corrected without exploration
Participation is driven by desire to give the teacher what they want
Student voice is tolerated, not genuinely valued
3: Teacher makes space for student contribution, some discussion, some open questions
Student voice is a feature of the lesson rather than its engine
Teacher's plan determines direction; student thinking is accommodated, not genuinely followed
4: Students regularly invited to reflect, reason, and question, not just answer
Teacher responds to student thinking with genuine curiosity
At least one moment where teacher visibly adjusts course based on what students say
5: Students drive significant portions of reflection and discussion, teacher facilitates rather than leads
Students build on each other's contributions without waiting for teacher validation
In student conversation, students reference specific ideas they raised in class as a normal part of how they learn here

1C5.name: Safe Space for Questions About Deen
1C5.focus: Do students feel safe enough to raise honest, difficult, or uncertain questions about faith, or do they mask doubts and comply with expected responses?

1: Students show visible anxiety or reluctance about expressing anything other than correct Islamic responses
Environment communicates, through teacher reactions or peer culture, that doubt or non-standard questions are unwelcome
Students may have questions but do not raise them
2: Questions about deen are tolerated but not actively invited
If a student raises a difficult question, teacher responds with an answer, not an invitation
Response closes the inquiry rather than opening it, students learn questions are managed, not explored
3: Students occasionally ask genuine faith questions and teacher receives them respectfully
Environment is not hostile but teacher's response remains primarily informational
Students do not feel the classroom is actively inviting their questions
4: At least one moment during the observation reveals a student raising a genuine faith question
Teacher responds with wisdom: doesn't rush to an answer, doesn't shut it down, doesn't make the student feel wrong to ask
Other students' responses suggest this happens regularly, it is not a one-off
5: Students raise deep, honest, personal questions about their deen as a normal part of classroom culture
Students ask identity questions, intellectual challenges, personal struggles without hesitation or embarrassment
If asked what makes this classroom safe to ask in, students can articulate specific reasons

1D.name: Pastoral Awareness
1D.description: Does the teacher notice and respond to the whole student, not just academic performance?

1D1.name: Awareness of Student Wellbeing
1D1.focus: Does the teacher notice the whole student? Focus on this individual teacher's murabbi practice, not the school's pastoral care system (Framework 2).

1: Focus is entirely academic, lesson delivery, behaviour management, task completion
A student who is visibly subdued, distracted, or emotionally struggling receives no acknowledgment
Pastoral care is not part of how this teacher understands their role
2: Notices when something seems wrong but delegates immediately without personal follow-up
The noticing is present; the murabbi response is absent
No private check-in or personal care shown in the moment of noticing
3: Some awareness of student wellbeing, a quiet check-in, a brief acknowledgment of difficulty
Pastoral care is additional to teaching rather than integrated with it
Responds when the signal is clear but does not proactively reach out
4: Actively notices changes in student demeanour, a student unusually quiet, one who seems to be carrying something
Makes brief, private contact: a quiet word, a desk check-in, an invitation after class
When asked who notices how they're doing beyond grades, students name this teacher
5: Students proactively bring this teacher their struggles, academic, personal, sometimes spiritual
At least one interaction reveals prior knowledge of a student's situation being carried forward
Students describe this teacher as someone who sees them, not just what they produce

1D2.name: Behaviour as Communication
1D2.focus: When a student misbehaves, does the teacher's first response ask what is happening for this student, or does it default immediately to consequence?

1: All misbehaviour treated as rule-breaking and managed with immediate consequence
No inquiry into cause, teacher has no apparent curiosity about why the student is behaving as they are
Repeat behaviour patterns are managed repeatedly without inquiry
2: Occasionally asks 'why?' after applying a consequence, but consequence always comes first
The question feels procedural rather than genuinely caring
Student's answer does not visibly change what happens next
3: In at least one instance, teacher pauses before responding, a brief exchange suggesting some curiosity
But inconsistent across the observation, other incidents are still consequence-first
Developing the habit of curiosity but has not made it their first instinct
4: When a student misbehaves, teacher's first visible response is curiosity, a quiet question ('everything okay?', 'what's going on?')
A moment of private attention before any consequence is applied
At least one incident clearly demonstrates this: teacher separates the behaviour from the student
5: Students themselves are visibly self-reflective about behaviour rather than defensive
Conversation between teacher and student is two-way: student participates in identifying what happened
Students describe this teacher as someone who always asks what's going on before reacting, and name this as why they trust them

1D3.name: Connection to Islamic Support Frameworks
1D3.focus: Does this teacher know and use the school's behaviour/pastoral frameworks, and connect them to Islamic values rather than applying them as purely secular procedures?

1: Unable to name or describe the school's behaviour frameworks (REAL Schools, IMP, or equivalent)
Approach to behaviour and pastoral care is entirely personal and unconnected to any school-wide system
No awareness of how the framework relates to Islamic values
2: Aware the frameworks exist and uses them when required, in a formal incident or when directed
No proactive engagement; cannot articulate how the framework connects to Islamic values
Applies procedure but has no Islamic rationale for it
3: Can describe the school's frameworks and applies them with some consistency
Application is procedural, follows the steps without connecting to Islamic concepts (tawbah, ihsan, accountability)
If asked how the framework relates to Islamic values, cannot answer clearly
4: Fluently uses the school's behaviour frameworks and can clearly articulate their connection to Islamic values
Understands how restorative consequences reflect tawbah, how root-cause inquiry reflects understanding the nafs
Applies the framework as an expression of Islamic pastoral care, not just school procedure
5: Models the integration of Islamic values and professional frameworks so naturally that students absorb both
In student conversation, students can articulate why they are held to account in Islamic terms
Framework has become a formation tool, students grow through it, not just comply

1D4.name: Communication with Parents
1D4.focus: Does this teacher communicate with parents as partners in Islamic formation, or is communication reactive, deficit-focused, and purely academic? Focus on this teacher's individual practice, not the school-wide strategy (Framework 2).

1: Communication is entirely reactive, parents hear from this teacher only when a problem arises
Communication is deficit-focused: behaviour incidents, missed work, academic underperformance only
No proactive outreach of any kind
2: Some proactive communication exists, end-of-term reports, academic updates, but academic in focus only
Does not communicate about Islamic formation, character development, or pastoral wellbeing
Parents are informed about their child as a student, not engaged as tarbiyyah partners
3: Communicates reasonably regularly and includes some positive messages alongside concerns
Islamic formation dimension is absent, parents receive academic and pastoral updates but no formation language
School-home communication is functional but not formative
4: At least one example shows a message going beyond academic reporting, a comment about character, positive Islamic engagement, or a pastoral concern raised with care
Teacher connects their communication approach to the idea of school-home formation partnership
Parents feel kept in the loop about formation, not just grades
5: Parents describe this teacher as a trusted murabbi figure, someone they'd contact about faith, character, or struggles
Named by parents specifically as communicating with warmth and genuine care
Parents feel they are on the same team, working together for the child's formation, not operating in separate spheres

F2.name: School Ethos & Culture
F2.centralQuestion: If a student spends six years in this school, what will they have absorbed about what it means to be Muslim, not from the Islamic Studies timetable, but from the air they breathed, the adults they watched, and the culture they were formed in?

2A.name: Leadership & Vision
2A.description: Is the Islamic formation vision clear, owned, and actively driven by leadership?

2A1.name: Clarity of Islamic Vision
2A1.focus: Is there a single, clearly articulated Islamic formation vision, and does everyone in the school know it, own it, and act on it?

1: No written or spoken Islamic formation vision exists at school level
Leadership cannot articulate a coherent answer to 'what are we trying to produce in our graduates?'
Staff in different departments have no shared language or direction for tarbiyyah
2: A vision statement exists in a policy document but is largely unknown to staff
Leadership can recite the vision but cannot explain how it shapes daily decisions
Ask three staff members: fewer than half give a recognisably similar answer
3: Leadership can articulate the vision clearly; some staff understand it, others have a vague sense
Vision shapes some decisions but not others consistently
Staff are aware of the vision but it has not become their own language
4: Staff across departments can articulate the tarbiyyah vision in their own words without prompting
Vision shapes visible decisions, resource allocation, event programming, staff development, behaviour frameworks
When asked what the school is trying to produce, staff and students give coherent, aligned answers
5: Students can articulate the school's Islamic formation vision and see it as shaping their own education
New staff describe being drawn to the school because of its articulated vision
The vision is self-correcting: staff notice and name when decisions drift from it

2A2.name: Leadership Models Islamic Character
2A2.focus: Is Islamic character visible in how leadership actually behaves, in corridor conversations, in difficult meetings, in moments of pressure, or only in formal speeches?

1: Islamic character is not observable in leadership's day-to-day manner, interactions are transactional or terse
Staff would not describe leadership as a model of Islamic conduct
Leadership's manner in informal settings is indistinguishable from a secular school leader
2: Leadership demonstrates Islamic values in formal, public moments but not consistently informally
Staff are aware of the gap between what leadership says and how it behaves
Sabr, shura, and rahma are spoken about but not consistently embodied in how decisions are made
3: Leadership is generally fair and respectful, Islamic character visible in major moments
Day-to-day interactions are decent but rarely recognisably Islamic in framing or manner
At least one member of leadership is named by staff as a genuine Islamic character model
4: Leadership consistently models sabr, adl, shura, and rahma across formal and informal settings
Staff cite specific examples of leadership embodying Islamic conduct, in how difficult news was shared, how conflicts were handled
Students and staff see Islamic leadership lived, not just spoken in assemblies
5: Staff explicitly describe their Islamic formation as influenced by leadership's example
Leadership's conduct is a reference point in staff culture: 'that's how we do things here' traces back to modelled behaviour
When leadership makes a mistake, they acknowledge it with tawadu, and this is noticed and described by staff

2A3.name: Tarbiyyah as a Genuine Strategic Priority
2A3.focus: Is tarbiyyah resourced, measured, and discussed alongside academic priorities, or is it the first thing dropped when time or money is tight?

1: Tarbiyyah is not mentioned in strategic planning, budget conversations, or performance review
Academic outcomes dominate all leadership conversations; Islamic formation is treated as automatic
No one has a specific brief or accountability for tarbiyyah outcomes
2: Tarbiyyah is mentioned in strategy documents and the principal speaks about it
No measurable outcomes, budget line, or formal staff time allocated to it
When tarbiyyah competes with an academic priority, tarbiyyah loses every time
3: Tarbiyyah has a named lead (e.g. Head of Tarbiyyah) and some allocated time
It competes with academic priorities for resource and does not always win
Progress is reviewed occasionally but not with the same rigour as academic KPIs
4: Tarbiyyah is budgeted, timetabled, tracked, and discussed at leadership level
At least one senior leader is accountable specifically for tarbiyyah outcomes
When trade-offs arise, tarbiyyah is not automatically deprioritised, the conversation happens explicitly
5: Staff experience tarbiyyah as the lens through which all school decisions are made, including academic ones
The language of formation runs through hiring decisions, curriculum design, event planning, parent communications
Tarbiyyah outcomes are reviewed with the same seriousness as academic results

2A4.name: Coherence of Programmes
2A4.focus: Can a random staff member explain how 5D Thinking, REAL Schools, the Murabbi Project, and Islamic Studies connect into a single formation vision? If not, that is your primary finding.

1: Staff cannot name all the programmes running in the school
No one can articulate how 5D Thinking, REAL Schools, and the Murabbi Project relate to each other
Each programme is managed separately with no shared framework
2: Staff are aware multiple programmes exist but see them as separate initiatives
Programme leads can describe their own strand but not how it connects to others
Students experience the programmes as disconnected, 'that's different from this'
3: Leadership can articulate the intended relationship between programmes
Some staff understand the connections; many do not
In practice, programmes operate semi-independently, overlap is not managed and duplication occurs
4: Most staff can articulate how 5D, REAL Schools, Murabbi, and Islamic Studies form a coherent whole
There is a shared map or framework that shows how programmes connect, and staff have been trained on it
When a new initiative is proposed, leadership asks 'where does this fit?' rather than adding it alongside
5: Students experience the school's programmes as a single, seamless formation journey rather than separate initiatives
A student asked about their Islamic education can describe how different elements connect, they have the map, not just the experiences
Staff from different programme areas collaborate naturally and plan across each other's strands

2A5.name: Scholarly / Spiritual Guidance
2A5.focus: Is there a trusted scholarly presence guiding the school's tarbiyyah direction, or are faith decisions made by leadership without scholarly grounding?

1: No guiding scholarly figure involved in tarbiyyah decisions, curriculum design, or spiritual culture
Faith-related questions are answered by management instinct, not Islamic knowledge
The absence of a Head of Faith is felt, no replacement has been found
2: Ad hoc scholarly input sought occasionally, a speaker brought in, a question sent to an external scholar
No ongoing relationship; scholarly guidance is reactive, not formative
Staff do not know who to consult when facing a complex faith-related pastoral or curriculum question
3: An external scholarly figure contributes in a specific, bounded way
Scholarly input shapes one area of the school's work but not the whole
Leadership would benefit from but does not have a trusted scholar who knows the school's context deeply
4: A trusted scholarly figure provides ongoing, contextualised guidance to leadership, not just content but direction
Staff know who the scholarly anchor is and can access that guidance through defined channels
Faith decisions, curriculum content, handling student doubt, tarbiyyah approach, are grounded in scholarly input
5: The scholarly presence is felt in school culture, staff and students reference scholarly guidance as part of everyday decision-making
Scholars engage not just with leadership but with teachers and students directly, building a culture of 'ilm
Staff describe the scholarly relationship as formative for their own Islamic development

2B.name: School Environment & Physical Culture
2B.description: Does walking into this school feel Islamic, not in a decorative sense, but in terms of what is felt, heard, and seen?

2B1.name: Visual Islamic Presence
2B1.focus: Does the physical environment communicate an active Islamic identity, connected to formation, not just decoration? Assess corridors, entrances, and common spaces.

1: Corridors and shared spaces communicate no Islamic identity, generic motivational posters, academic displays
A visitor would not know this is an Islamic school from the physical environment alone
Islamic content is absent or so minimal it has no presence
2: Islamic content exists but is purely decorative, Arabic calligraphy, stock Quranic ayaat posters
Displays are old, faded, or clearly unchanged across the year
Students walk past Islamic content without engaging with it, it has become invisible
3: Islamic content is visible and present throughout, ayaat, hadith, character reminders
Some displays are connected to school themes but many are generic
Content is not actively integrated into school life, teachers don't reference displays, students don't contribute
4: The physical environment actively communicates this school's Islamic formation journey
Displays are connected to current learning, recently updated, and in some cases created with student input
A visitor can read the walls and understand something about what this community is working on spiritually
5: Students engage with and contribute to the Islamic environment, displays include their own reflections and creations
Visitors consistently remark on the distinct character of the space without prompting
The visual environment reflects the school's living culture, not a static aspiration pinned to walls

2B2.name: Sound and Rhythm of the School Day
2B2.focus: Does the Islamic rhythm of the day feel real, or does it feel like a procedural interruption to the academic schedule?

1: No Islamic rhythm distinguishes this school day from a secular one
The adhan, salah, and adhkar are absent from shared school experience
Transitions and breaks have no Islamic framing
2: Salah and adhkar exist on the timetable but are treated as administrative interruptions
Students move to salah as they move to any lesson, without preparation, presence, or spiritual weight
The rhythm exists on paper; it is not felt
3: Islamic rhythm is present and consistent, the adhan is sounded, salah happens at its time
The rhythm is maintained but not cultivated, some staff treat it with reverence, others manage it procedurally
Students experience the rhythm as a schedule feature, not a spiritual anchor
4: The Islamic rhythm of the school day is felt, transitions include dhikr, salah is treated as a sacred pause
Staff model attentiveness to the rhythm, they stop what they're doing, they prepare
Students experience the rhythm as meaningful anchors in the day, not as interruptions
5: Students protect the rhythm themselves, reminding each other, settling without being told, staying for voluntary dhikr after salah
The rhythm has become a marker of identity: 'this is how we do things here'
A visitor arriving during a salah transition would experience something unmistakably distinct from a secular school

2B3.name: Salah Culture
2B3.focus: The question is not whether salah happens, it is whether salah is treated as formation or administration.

1: Salah is not a meaningful part of school practice, absent, treated as optional, or so poorly managed it carries no spiritual weight
Staff do not pray with students
The masjid or prayer space is a room, not a sacred environment
2: Salah is scheduled and happens, but as a tick, not a practice
No attention to quality, concentration, or meaning
Students file in and out efficiently; spiritual preparation and reflection are absent
3: Salah happens at its time with reasonable attendance and some sense of order
Some staff pray alongside students and guide with brief reminders about presence
Inconsistent across year groups, some classes approach salah with reverence; others do not
4: Students are guided in concentration (khushu); teachers pray alongside students as a norm
Brief pre-salah reminder or du'a is a consistent practice; Hamdala follows
Reflection on salah, its meaning, its connection to daily life, is part of tarbiyyah conversations
5: Students are observed in voluntary dhikr or supplication around salah time without being prompted
Salah has become personal practice, not just school practice, students describe it as the most grounding part of their day
Salah culture is maintained even when leadership is absent, students lead, students remind each other

2B4.name: Language and Communication
2B4.focus: Does the school's official and informal communication use Islamic language meaningfully, or is it formulaic (Bismillah on every email but secular in substance)?

1: All school communication, newsletters, emails, announcements, signage, is entirely secular in framing
No Islamic language present in how the school speaks to itself and to its community
Islamic phrases, when present, are absent from any substantive communication
2: Islamic phrases appear formulaically (Assalamualaikum, Inshallah, Alhamdulillah) but the substance of communication is entirely secular
Islamic language is performative rather than reflective of genuine Islamic culture
Formal communications are indistinguishable from a non-Islamic school if you remove the opening salaam
3: Islamic language is present and generally meaningful in formal communications
Some communications are genuinely Islamic in framing, connecting school events to Islamic purpose
Informal communication in staffroom and corridors uses Islamic language inconsistently
4: Islamic language permeates school communications naturally and meaningfully, expressing who this community is
Formal communications connect school life to Islamic purpose: achievements framed as amanah, difficulties as tests, community life as ummah
Islamic language in informal settings is consistent and unselfconscious across most staff
5: Students adopt Islamic language naturally in their own communication, with peers, in schoolwork, in formal settings
The language of the school has become the language of its students, it reflects genuine internalisation
Visitors note the distinctiveness of how this community speaks to and about itself

2B5.name: Feel of Transitions and Informal Time
2B5.focus: Culture lives in the spaces between structured programmes, the corridors, the lunch hall, the sports field. What do these spaces tell you about what is actually being formed?

1: Corridors and breaks are indistinguishable from a non-Islamic school, peer dynamics, language, and behaviour reflect no Islamic formation
Social hierarchies, exclusion, and unkind peer dynamics are visible and unaddressed
Islamic identity is invisible in informal spaces
2: Some positive norms exist in informal settings but have no Islamic framing
Students are generally decent, but this could be any well-managed school
Islamic identity is not visible in how students carry themselves or relate to each other in unstructured time
3: Some Islamic norms are visible in informal settings, salam greetings, students helping each other
Inconsistent across year groups and locations
Staff presence in informal spaces is variable, some engage warmly, others supervise at distance
4: Students' behaviour in informal settings reflects Islamic formation, adab, ukhuwwah, and respect visible without enforcement
Students greet each other with salam naturally; helping behaviour is common; unkindness is rare and noticed
Staff move through informal spaces with warmth and purpose, their presence is relational, not just supervisory
5: Visitors comment on the distinctive informal culture without prompting
Students who transfer from other schools describe the informal culture as noticeably different
Islamic identity is visible in how students carry themselves and relate, even when no staff are watching

2C.name: Pastoral Care & Behaviour Framework
2C.description: Does the school's approach to student wellbeing and behaviour reflect Islamic values, or is it secular best practice with Islamic language added?

2C1.name: Islamic Overlay on REAL Schools Framework
2C1.focus: Does the school's behaviour management approach carry an Islamic soul, or is it a secular framework with Islamic words retrofitted?

1: Behaviour management has no Islamic framing, sanctions and consequences operate in a purely secular register
Concepts like tawbah, ihsan, and responsibility before Allah are entirely absent
Staff apply consequences as rule enforcement; Islamic dimension does not exist in practice
2: Staff use the behaviour system but with no connection to Islamic concepts of accountability
Islamic language occasionally used in pastoral conversations, 'Allah is watching' as a warning, but feels retrofitted
Staff cannot articulate how the behaviour framework relates to Islamic values
3: Framework is applied consistently by most staff
Some staff connect it to Islamic values naturally; most apply it procedurally
Training has not systematically established the Islamic rationale for the restorative approach
4: Staff understand the framework as an Islamic framework, restorative consequences understood as reflecting tawbah and ihsan
In pastoral conversations, staff connect consequences to Islamic responsibility naturally, not formulaically
Training has established the Islamic rationale, staff can articulate why this approach aligns with Islamic values
5: Students articulate consequences in Islamic terms, accountability before Allah is a lived reality, not a phrase
When asked why they are held to account, students connect it to their identity as Muslims, not just school rules
The framework is a formation tool, students grow through it, not just comply with it

2C2.name: Root-Cause Approach to Behaviour
2C2.focus: Does the school treat misbehaviour as communication, something to understand, or purely as a rule violation to be managed?

1: Behaviour issues managed only as rule violations, sanction applied, record made, case closed
No staff member asks what might be happening for the student behind the presenting behaviour
Repeat behaviour patterns are managed repeatedly without inquiry into cause
2: Surface-level curiosity in some pastoral conversations but not systemic
Individual staff may ask 'what's going on?' but this is personal style, not school culture
Behaviour data tracked but not analysed for patterns or root causes at a school level
3: Some staff consistently look for root causes; others do not
Pastoral care is available but uneven, quality of response depends on which teacher a student happens to go to
The school is aware of the gap but has not systematically shifted its approach
4: Across the school, behaviour is understood as communication, first response is curiosity, then consequence
Behaviour data is used to identify patterns and trigger pastoral intervention before issues escalate
Staff have language and training for root-cause inquiry: 'What might this behaviour be telling us?'
5: Students themselves articulate the root causes of their behaviour, self-awareness is a formation outcome
When misbehaving, students are not defensive, they have been formed to understand themselves
The school can point to specific cases where root-cause pastoral care changed a student's trajectory

2C3.name: Student Support and Mentoring (Murabbi Culture)
2C3.focus: Is there a genuine murabbi culture, multiple adults who see themselves as formative mentors, or is student support concentrated in one person and therefore fragile?

1: Student support is crisis-driven and reactive, pastoral care only triggered when a serious problem emerges
No adult in the school describes their role in terms of long-term formation mentorship
Students do not know who to go to with a faith or personal struggle
2: Some pastoral care exists but concentrated in one person or department
Most teachers see their role as subject delivery; pastoral care is someone else's job
Students with struggles know to go to one designated person, the culture is not distributed
3: Some teachers embrace a murabbi identity individually but it is not a school-wide expectation
Some students have an adult they trust; many do not
The Murabbi Project exists outwardly but the internal murabbi culture among staff is underdeveloped
4: A genuine murabbi culture exists, multiple staff across departments see themselves as formative mentors
Students know who their murabbi is and are known by more than one adult in the school
The culture is embedded in role expectations, not just encouraged but expected and supported
5: Students can name their murabbi, seek them out proactively, and describe the impact of the relationship on their growth
The murabbi culture is visible in the rhythm of the day, check-ins, walking to salah, sitting with students at lunch
New staff are formed into the murabbi culture, it is part of how this school understands itself

2C4.name: Wellbeing and Mental Health Through an Islamic Lens
2C4.focus: Is student wellbeing addressed through an integrated Islamic and professional framework, or is mental health support entirely secular, with Islam absent from the conversation?

1: Wellbeing support is entirely secular, counselling and pastoral care draw on no Islamic framework
Islamic spiritual practices (dhikr, tawakkul, muhasabah) are never referenced in wellbeing contexts
Students experiencing spiritual struggle have no Islamic language or support structure for it
2: Islamic language occasionally appears in pastoral contexts, 'make du'a', 'have tawakkul', but not as a systematic framework
No staff member equipped to integrate Islamic spiritual tools with professional wellbeing support
Students who present with spiritual doubt or crisis are referred as if it were a generic mental health issue
3: Some staff can integrate Islamic and professional wellbeing frameworks, particularly those with Islamic Studies backgrounds
This is personal competence, not institutional design
The school is aware of the gap but has not yet built a systematic integrated approach
4: Student wellbeing is addressed through an integrated Islamic and professional framework, spiritual and emotional health understood as connected
Staff equipped with tools drawing on both Islamic tradition (muhasabah, tawakkul, sabr) and professional wellbeing frameworks
Spiritual crises are treated as real pastoral needs with appropriate Islamic support, not just mental health referrals
5: Students understand their emotional and spiritual wellbeing as inseparable, Islamic self-care practices are part of their toolkit
Students describe using muhasabah, du'a, and the Quran as genuine coping tools, not just things they are told to do
The school is a safe place for students to name spiritual doubt, and know they will receive Islamic pastoral care

2C5.name: Inclusion and Dignity
2C5.focus: Does the school community embody an active culture of inclusion grounded in Islamic values, or is safety achieved through management rather than genuine brotherhood and sisterhood?

1: Visible pockets of exclusion, bullying, or marginalisation present and not being addressed
Certain students, by year group, ability, or social profile, are clearly outsiders in the culture
Islamic values of brotherhood and sisterhood are referenced but not operative in peer dynamics
2: School is generally safe but Islamic brotherhood/sisterhood culture is absent
Exclusion is managed when it becomes a problem but not actively countered through formation
Peer dynamics reflect social hierarchies that are unchallenged by the school's Islamic values
3: School is generally safe and positive
Islamic brotherhood/sisterhood is spoken about and valued but not systematically cultivated
Some year groups have strong peer culture; others have social dynamics that undermine inclusion
4: The school community embodies an active culture of inclusion grounded in Islamic values, every student belongs to the ummah is a lived reality
Peer inclusion is actively cultivated through seating, grouping, student leadership, and pastoral care
Exclusion is named and addressed in Islamic terms, as a violation of ukhuwwah, not just as a behaviour issue
5: Students actively include and advocate for each other as an expression of their Islamic identity, without being prompted
Students who are socially vulnerable are protected by the peer culture, not just by staff
When exclusion occurs, other students name it and address it, inclusion has become a student-owned value

2D.name: Staff Formation & Culture
2D.description: Are the staff themselves on a journey of Islamic formation, or are they expected to form students in a faith they are not actively developing in themselves?

2D1.name: Staff Islamic Formation
2D1.focus: Are staff growing in their own Islamic formation as part of working at this school, or is formation expected to happen at home, on their own time?

1: No investment in staff's own Islamic development, no ta'leem, no Islamic CPD, no spiritual dimension to staff life
Staff are expected to deliver Islamic content without support for their own formation
Staff Islamic formation is treated as a private matter unrelated to professional life
2: Occasional Islamic input for staff, a visiting scholar once a year, a Ramadan programme, but ad hoc
Staff value the occasions but cannot describe a programme or journey of formation
No staff member feels accountable for their own Islamic growth as part of their professional role
3: Some Islamic formation for staff exists, regular ta'leem, Islamic Studies input, or reflection sessions
Valuable but not connected to a coherent framework, staff don't experience it as a journey
Take-up is variable, some staff are engaged; others feel it is not relevant to them
4: Staff have regular, meaningful Islamic formation opportunities clearly part of working at this school
Formation is connected to the school's tarbiyyah mission, staff understand their development as inseparable from what they are trying to produce in students
Engagement is high and the programme has a coherent shape, it is building something, not just offering isolated sessions
5: Staff describe their Islamic formation as one of the most valued aspects of working at this school
Formation is visible in staff practice, the ta'leem is not just attended, it is absorbed and referenced
Senior staff can articulate how their Islamic formation has shaped their teaching, pastoral care, or leadership

2D2.name: Staff as Murabbis
2D2.focus: Do all staff, not just Islamic Studies teachers, see themselves as formative agents in students' Islamic development?

1: Staff see their role purely as subject delivery
The murabbi identity is associated only with Islamic Studies teachers, or with no one
A Maths teacher asked 'what is your role in a student's tarbiyyah?' would not have a meaningful answer
2: Some staff embrace a murabbi identity individually, but this is personal character, not a school-wide expectation
No staff role description, appraisal, or induction includes tarbiyyah as a professional responsibility
Most staff see formation as someone else's job
3: The school articulates the expectation that all staff are murabbis, it is named in culture and communication
In practice, a minority of staff embody it; the majority are still primarily subject-focused
The gap between aspiration and reality is known but not yet closed through training or accountability
4: School culture expects and supports every staff member to be a murabbi, embedded in role descriptions and appraisal conversations
Most staff can articulate how their subject or role contributes to Islamic formation
Formation conversations happen across departments, not just in Islamic Studies
5: Students experience all staff as murabbis, the identity is consistent across every adult in the school
A student asked to name an adult outside Islamic Studies who has shaped their Islamic character names one without hesitation
Staff hold each other to the murabbi identity and celebrate examples of it

2D3.name: Teacher Knowledge of the Behaviour Framework
2D3.focus: Can all staff confidently apply the school's behaviour frameworks, and connect them to Islamic values, or is implementation variable and poorly understood?

1: Many staff cannot name or describe the school's behaviour frameworks (IMP, REAL Schools, or equivalent)
Application is entirely personal, each teacher manages behaviour according to their own preference
Students experience very different standards in different classrooms and are aware of this
2: Staff know the frameworks in theory but application is inconsistent and unconfident
Many staff default to their own approach when the formal framework feels difficult
The Islamic rationale for the framework is not known or articulated by most staff
3: Most staff know and apply the framework reasonably well
Inconsistency remains, particularly between experienced and newer staff, and between departments
The Islamic rationale for the framework is known by some, not most
4: All staff confidently apply the IMP/REAL/consequence frameworks
Staff can connect the framework to Islamic values of fairness, restoration, and growth, not just as school procedure
Inconsistency is minimal, students experience a consistent approach across their school day
5: Staff adapt the frameworks fluidly to complex situations, they are tools, not scripts
In conversation with any staff member, the Islamic rationale for the approach is articulated naturally
Students notice and value the consistency, they describe the school's approach to behaviour as fair and trust-based

2D4.name: Collaborative Tarbiyyah Culture Among Staff
2D4.focus: Do staff share a language and approach to Islamic formation, and plan across each other's areas, or does each teacher operate in isolation?

1: Staff operate in silos, no shared language, no cross-departmental conversation about formation
Islamic Studies teachers are the only ones who discuss tarbiyyah; other departments are entirely disconnected
No forum, meeting structure, or culture that brings staff together around formation
2: Limited cross-departmental conversation about tarbiyyah, typically only when a student issue triggers it
No shared language across departments; 'tarbiyyah' and '5D Thinking' are known in some areas, unknown in others
Formation planning is done department by department with no coordination
3: Some cross-departmental tarbiyyah conversation exists, typically driven by one or two motivated staff
Shared language is developing but not consistent
Some collaborative planning happens but is not systematic or regular
4: Staff across all departments share a language and approach to Islamic formation, they plan together and hold each other accountable
Cross-departmental tarbiyyah conversations are structured into the school year, not just reactive
A History teacher and an Islamic Studies teacher can describe how they coordinate on a shared formation goal
5: Staff culture is itself a model of the ummah, collaborative, mutually accountable, and visibly purposeful
Collaboration is visible to students: they experience the school's approach as unified
Staff describe the collaborative culture as one of the most distinctive and motivating features of working here

2D5.name: Staff Wellbeing and Sense of Purpose
2D5.focus: Do staff feel supported, valued, and part of a meaningful mission, or are they carrying the tarbiyyah agenda as an additional burden on top of an already heavy workload?

1: Low morale, high turnover, or staff who explicitly describe the tarbiyyah agenda as an additional burden
Staff feel the Islamic formation expectations are unrealistic given their workload
No investment in staff wellbeing beyond statutory requirements
2: Staff are generally committed but show signs of change management fatigue, multiple initiatives, little sequencing, limited capacity
Some staff feel overwhelmed by the volume of change (5D, REAL Schools, Murabbi, Turkey training) running simultaneously
Good intentions but poor capacity management, staff are burning out from doing too much
3: Staff are broadly positive about the school's mission but workload is a real concern
The tarbiyyah agenda is valued but not yet sequenced in a way that feels manageable
Leadership is aware of the tension and is working to address it
4: Staff feel genuinely supported, valued, and part of a meaningful mission
The school's Islamic formation agenda energises staff rather than depleting them, it is the reason they are here
Workload is managed with awareness of formation fatigue, initiatives are sequenced, not piled
5: Staff actively advocate for and recruit others to the school's mission
Purpose is a driving force, not a slogan, staff describe this school as a contribution to the ummah, and mean it
Staff retention is high; departures are felt as genuine losses to the community

2E.name: Parent & Community Engagement
2E.description: Does the school's Islamic formation reach beyond the school gate, and do parents see themselves as partners in this work?

2E1.name: Parent Understanding of the School's Islamic Vision
2E1.focus: Do parents understand what the school is trying to do in their child's Islamic formation, or did they choose the school for vague Islamic values and have no active engagement with its vision?

1: Parents have no awareness of the school's tarbiyyah approach beyond 'it's an Islamic school'
The school communicates with parents about events and academics but never about Islamic formation vision
Parents are customers of an Islamic education service, not partners in a formation mission
2: Parents chose the school for Islamic values and have a general sense of its ethos
They are not engaged with the specific vision, its components, or their role within it
Most parents could not name the school's programmes (5D, Murabbi, REAL Schools) or explain what they are
3: Some parents are engaged with the school's Islamic formation vision, particularly those who attend events
The majority see the school as a service provider rather than a community they are part of
Communication about Islamic formation happens but is not consistent or widely received
4: Parents are regularly engaged with the school's Islamic formation vision and understand their role in continuing it at home
Formation-focused parent communication is a regular feature of school life, not just academic reporting
At a parent event, most attendees can describe what the school is trying to achieve and why
5: Parents articulate the school's tarbiyyah vision in their own words and actively reinforce it at home
Parents see themselves as partners in formation, not consumers of education
Some parents have become advocates for the school's approach in the wider community

2E2.name: Consistency Between School and Home
2E2.focus: Is there active effort to align Islamic formation between the school environment and the home, or do students live two different formation lives that never speak to each other?

1: No attempt to align Islamic formation between school and home
Parents receive academic communications; nothing extends the school's tarbiyyah approach into family life
Students experience school and home as entirely separate with no shared formation language
2: School communicates with parents about behaviour but not about Islamic formation specifically
No structured effort to help parents understand the school's approach so they can reinforce it
The Murabbi Project exists outwardly but has not systematically penetrated family life
3: Some connection between school and home, parents who attend events understand the school's approach better
No systematic mechanism for sharing formation language, tools, or expectations with all parents
Quality of school-home alignment depends on whether individual parents choose to engage
4: The school actively supports parents in creating an Islamic home environment consistent with the school's approach
Formation language is shared with parents, they receive the same vocabulary and frameworks their children are using
The Murabbi Project reaches into family life, parents report changing their approach at home as a result
5: Parents describe the school's home-school formation work as transformative for their family, not just their child
Home and school are genuinely aligned: language, practices, and values are continuous across contexts
Students describe their family and school as reinforcing each other, formation is holistic, not compartmentalised

2E3.name: Parent Education and Empowerment
2E3.focus: Does the school equip parents as formative agents, not just inform them about school events? Assess actual reach and impact of the Murabbi Project, not just its existence.

1: No parent Islamic formation programmes or community engagement
Parents are invited to open evenings and sports days, nothing equips them for their role in tarbiyyah
The Murabbi Project has not reached into parent formation
2: Occasional parent events exist (a talk, a Ramadan gathering) but no coherent formation programme
Events are appreciated but attendance is variable and impact is not measured or followed up
Content is informational, parents leave knowing more, not necessarily doing more differently
3: The Murabbi Project provides some structured parent engagement
Reach is limited, the same parents attend repeatedly; the majority are not engaged
Content is informational rather than formative
4: Regular, well-attended parent seminars on Islamic parenting and tarbiyyah, parents feel equipped and supported, not just informed
The school tracks engagement and actively pursues harder-to-reach parents
After a parent seminar, parents report specific changes in how they approach faith formation at home
5: Parents have become ambassadors and facilitators, some co-delivering or contributing to parent formation programmes
The parent community itself is a formation community, parents support each other in tarbiyyah
Strong parent formation visibly amplifies student formation, the link is observable

2E4.name: Community Voices in School Life
2E4.focus: Is the school embedded in the wider Muslim community, drawing on alumni, scholars, and family wisdom, or is it an island that operates in isolation?

1: The community is not involved in school values, culture, or programming
No alumni engagement, no community scholars, no family voices in school culture
The school is experienced as a professional institution that delivers education to the community, not as a community institution
2: Community is occasionally invited to events, a speaker, a fundraiser, but has no voice in school culture
Alumni are not engaged; their formation outcomes are not tracked or drawn on
Community figures know the school exists but do not feel connected to its mission
3: Some community involvement exists, community figures occasionally contribute, alumni are sometimes invited back
Involvement is event-based rather than embedded
The school would like to be a community hub but has not yet built the structures to achieve this
4: Community figures, alumni, and parents are active contributors to the school's tarbiyyah culture
Alumni come back and speak to students about how their formation at school shaped their life
The school is experienced as a hub by at least part of the community, people associate it with Islamic formation, not just education
5: The school is experienced as a community institution, alumni, scholars, and families feel genuine ownership of its mission
Community wisdom actively shapes the school's approach through genuine involvement, not just consultation
The school's impact is felt beyond its gates: community members credit it with contributing to the Islamic formation of the neighbourhood

2E5.name: School-Parent Relationship Quality
2E5.focus: Is the school-parent relationship warm, collaborative, and trust-based, or transactional, reactive, and deficit-focused?

1: School-parent relationship is reactive and deficit-focused, parents hear from the school mainly when there is a problem
Parents feel managed, not partnered; the relationship is formal and distant
No infrastructure for warm, ongoing communication about formation
2: Regular communication exists but it is transactional and academic, newsletters, reports, grade portals
The relationship is cordial but surface-level; communication is one-directional: school to parent
Parents don't feel they know the school and the school doesn't know its families
3: Some warmth exists in the school-parent relationship, parents who engage feel known
But engagement is opt-in; the school has not created structures that draw all parents in
Quality of the relationship depends heavily on individual staff members' personalities
4: School-parent relationship is warm, collaborative, and built on mutual trust, parents feel seen and heard, not managed
Multiple structured touchpoints throughout the year allow for genuine conversation, not just parents' evenings
Parents feel able to raise concerns without fear and to celebrate without formality
5: Parents describe their relationship with the school as one of the most meaningful partnerships in their child's upbringing
Parents and staff know each other, not just as roles but as people
The school-parent relationship is itself a formation relationship: parents grow in their understanding of tarbiyyah through connection with the school

F3.name: The Curriculum
F3.centralQuestion: Is the Islamic curriculum producing students who know Islam, or students who are becoming Muslim? There is a profound difference between a student who can answer exam questions about Aqeedah and a student whose certainty in Allah shapes how they live.

3A.name: Islamic Studies, Adab & Quran - Coherence & Quality
3A.description: Is the dedicated Islamic curriculum purposeful, well-sequenced, and formation-oriented?

3A1.name: Coherence Across Year Groups
3A1.focus: Is there a clear, sequenced Islamic knowledge framework that students progress through, or do topics repeat randomly and gaps appear between year groups?

1: No sequenced Islamic curriculum, topics chosen by individual teachers based on preference or habit
Significant repetition across year groups: students cover the same Aqeedah basics in Years 7, 9, and 11
Significant gaps: important areas (fiqh of contemporary issues, eschatology, Islamic ethics) are never covered
2: Some sequencing exists informally, teachers are aware of what was covered in previous years, but not documented
Curriculum coherence depends on individual teachers' knowledge of each other's content
When a teacher leaves, the sequencing often collapses
3: A written scheme of work exists across year groups; some sequencing is apparent
Not consistently applied, individual teachers supplement, replace, or reorder content based on personal preferences
Students may have significant repetition in some areas and gaps in others depending on their class
4: There is a clear, coherent Islamic knowledge framework sequenced across all year levels
A Year 10 Islamic Studies lesson visibly assumes and extends Year 9 content
Teachers can explain how their year group fits into the whole; students understand their learning journey
5: Students articulate their learning journey, they know where they have come from and where they are going in their Islamic education
The curriculum is treated as a living document: reviewed, updated, and improved based on outcomes
Students experience Islamic education as a building journey, not a series of standalone lessons

3A2.name: Formation vs Information
3A2.focus: Are Islamic Studies lessons primarily content delivery, or do they use knowledge as a vehicle for formation? The question is not whether information is taught but whether it is expected to change the student.

1: Islamic Studies lessons are entirely information delivery, content transmitted, comprehension checked, lesson ends
No moment of reflection, application, or personal connection is designed into lessons
Students could pass a knowledge test and be entirely unchanged by it
2: Brief reflection moments exist, 'what does this mean for us?', but incidental and underdeveloped
Formation intent exists in teachers' minds but is not designed into lesson structure
Reflection is a token addition at the end of a content-heavy lesson
3: Some lessons use a structured reflection or application component
Quality is inconsistent, some teachers create genuine formation moments; others deliver content with a token reflection
The Learn - Reflect - React framework is used by some teachers but not embedded school-wide
4: Lessons consistently use a structured approach, students are regularly asked how knowledge applies to their lives
Formation moments are designed, not improvised, lesson plans include application and reflection as core components
A lesson observer can see clear formation intent in lesson design, not just delivery
5: Students demonstrate formation outcomes in behaviour and choices outside of Islamic Studies, knowledge has become character
When asked about an Islamic Studies lesson, students describe what it made them decide, not just what they learned
The school can point to observable student formation outcomes that trace back to specific curriculum choices

3A3.name: Adab Programme Quality and Use
3A3.focus: Is the Adab programme a meaningful formation tool, or is it treated as a filler by teachers and students alike?

1: No functioning Adab programme, or it exists on the timetable but has no meaningful content or delivery
Teachers and students treat Adab classes as a non-lesson: phones out, admin done, no real learning
No one, including leadership, can describe what the Adab programme is trying to achieve
2: Adab classes exist and are attended but content is not well-designed for formation
Topics are generic ('be respectful', 'have good manners') without depth, progression, or student engagement
Teachers are not equipped to deliver Adab meaningfully, classes feel like pastoral time rather than formation
3: Adab classes have genuine content, Islamic character topics, reflection activities, group discussion
Some teachers deliver it well; others struggle or improvise
Students generally cooperate but do not experience Adab as significant in their formation journey, no clear progression across year groups
4: Adab classes are a meaningful part of student formation, content is intentional, discussions are real, students engage genuinely
There is a coherent scope and sequence, what is covered in Year 7 is different from and builds on what comes later
Students reference Adab class discussions in other settings, it reaches beyond the timetable slot
5: Students reference Adab class discussions months later as things that genuinely shaped their thinking
The Adab programme has visible formation impact: students use the language and concepts from Adab in everyday conversation
Teachers delivering Adab feel equipped and confident, the programme is a professional asset, not an awkward addition

3A4.name: Quran Programme - Depth Beyond Recitation
3A4.focus: Is the Quran programme producing students who have a living, reflective relationship with the Quran, or students who can recite but have no engagement with meaning?

1: Quran classes focus entirely on recitation and tajweed, meaning is never engaged
Students assessed on recitation quality only; no expectation of engagement with meaning
The Quran is taught as a text to be performed, not a guide to be lived
2: Meaning is occasionally touched on, a brief translation, a word explanation, but not in a designed or consistent way
Tadabbur is entirely absent, students are never asked to reflect on the Quran as relevant to their lives
Quran teachers are resistant to any engagement with meaning as their responsibility
3: Some reflection on Quran meaning occurs, teachers occasionally share meaning, context, or a brief reflection
Not systematic, depends on individual teachers and the day
The tadabbur gap is real and known within the school, named as something to address but not yet actioned
4: Quran learning integrates meaning, reflection, and application, students encounter the Quran as guidance for their lives
Tadabbur is a structured component of the programme, students regularly reflect on selected ayaat in relation to their lives
Quran teachers and Islamic Studies teachers are coordinating, meaning is not entirely absent from one programme when present in another
5: Students independently connect Quranic verses to their experiences and decisions, the Quran has become a living guide
Students can share an ayah that has been particularly meaningful to them and explain why, and this is normal conversation, not performance
Students' relationship with the Quran extends beyond school: they read, reflect, and return to it independently

3A5.name: Applied Islam - Practical Formation
3A5.focus: Are students equipped to navigate modern Muslim life through an Islamic lens, or does the curriculum produce knowledge of classical texts with no connection to the real challenges students face?

1: No structured programme connecting Islamic knowledge to real-life application
Contemporary Muslim challenges (social media, relationships, mental health, career, identity) are entirely absent from Islamic Studies content
Students learn fiqh in classical terms with no bridge to the decisions they make daily
2: Some applied content exists but sporadic, individual teachers bring in contemporary examples when they feel it's relevant
Not designed into the curriculum: applied Islam is improvised, not planned
Students don't have a framework for thinking Islamically about new situations, they need to be told the ruling
3: Applied Islam content appears in some year groups or modules but is not systematic across the curriculum
The Applied Islam Course addresses this but has a scalability problem, it depends on one person
A Year 9 student may have excellent applied content while a Year 11 student has none
