4: Students regularly engage with how their deen applies to real decisions, technology, relationships, career, mental health, and identity addressed through an Islamic lens
Applied Islam is designed into the curriculum across year groups, not dependent on one person or one course
Students have a basic framework for thinking Islamically about new situations, not just a list of rulings
5: Students demonstrate the ability to derive Islamic guidance for novel situations independently, applied Islam is a thinking skill, not a lesson type
Students approach new challenges by asking 'what does my deen say?' rather than waiting to be told
When asked about a challenge they are facing, students can articulate an Islamic perspective on it with genuine depth

3B.name: Islamic Integration Across the General Curriculum
3B.description: Does Islam appear in Maths, Science, English, and other subjects, or does it disappear the moment students leave the Islamic Studies room?

3B1.name: 5D Framework Integration
3B1.focus: Is tawheed-integrated teaching a reality across the general curriculum, or practiced by a few enthusiasts and ignored by most?

1: No evidence of 5D or tawheed-based integration in non-Islamic subjects
Subject teachers entirely unaware of the framework, or aware but have made no attempt to apply it
Science, Maths, English, and Humanities are taught in a purely secular intellectual frame
2: Isolated attempts by individual teachers, one History teacher references Muslim scholars, one Science teacher mentions Allah's creation, but without school-wide approach
Most general subject teachers cannot explain what 5D Thinking is or how it applies to their subject
The 5D team exists but has not yet embedded the approach into daily practice across the school
3: Some teachers attempt integration consistently and effectively
The school has the infrastructure (5D team, training programme, Turkey PD) but implementation is still limited to a minority of staff
Students experience Islamic integration in some subjects and not others, it depends entirely on which teacher they have
4: 5D Thinking is embedded across multiple subject areas, Islamic integration observable across at least 3 non-Islamic subjects during the visit
Most general subject teachers can describe how they integrate 5D Thinking into their planning
The approach is recognisable: students can identify the Islamic dimension of their general subjects when asked
5: Students apply 5D thinking independently across subjects, they make the connections themselves, not just when the teacher offers them
Ask a student from a Science or History class: 'what does your faith have to do with this subject?', they answer substantively without hesitation
5D Thinking has become the students' own intellectual lens, not a layer the teacher adds

3B2.name: Cross-Curricular Islamic Perspectives
3B2.focus: Are Islamic intellectual tradition, Muslim contributions to knowledge, and Islamic ethical perspectives visible across the general curriculum, or does Islam disappear the moment students leave Islamic Studies?

1: Non-Islamic subject teachers have no language or tools for Islamic integration
Muslim contributions to science, mathematics, philosophy, and history are entirely absent from general subject teaching
Students experience academic learning and Islamic learning as completely separate domains
2: Occasional references to Islam in academic subjects but unplanned and inconsistent
One teacher may mention Ibn Battuta; another may reference Al-Khawarizmi, but these are individual choices, not cultural norm
No student could describe a pattern of Islamic intellectual tradition across their subjects
3: Some teachers across subjects confidently reference Islamic intellectual tradition and Muslim contributions
This is a growing culture rather than an embedded one, visible in some classrooms, absent from many
Students notice and appreciate it when it happens but experience it as an exception, not the norm
4: Teachers across subjects regularly reference Islamic intellectual tradition, Muslim contributions, and Islamic ethical perspectives relevant to their discipline
Students encounter the Islamic intellectual tradition in non-Islamic lessons as a matter of course, it is expected, not exotic
A student reviewing their school week can identify multiple moments across subjects where their Islamic heritage was part of the lesson
5: Students surface Islamic intellectual tradition independently in academic work, they don't wait for the teacher to make the connection
In a student essay or project, Islamic perspectives appear naturally, not because required but because part of how the student thinks
Students describe their academic learning as strengthening rather than competing with their iman

3B3.name: Islamic Values in Assessment and Expectations
3B3.focus: Is academic achievement communicated within an Islamic framework, or does the school measure success in purely secular academic terms?

1: Success defined entirely in secular academic terms, grades, rankings, university entry
No Islamic framing of academic purpose, effort, excellence, or achievement is not connected to amanah, ibadah, or khilafah
The school's academic culture and its Islamic culture are entirely separate registers
2: Some acknowledgment of Islamic values in expectations, 'work hard, it's an amanah', but not embedded in assessment language or school-wide culture
The message is occasional and inconsistent; the dominant academic culture is competitive and secular
The Islamic narrative and the assessment system are two separate worlds
3: Islamic values appear in how some teachers talk about academic work, some frame effort as ibadah and achievement as amanah
But not embedded in school-wide assessment practice, reports, targets, and recognition still operate in a secular frame
The gap between the Islamic narrative and the assessment system is noticeable
4: Academic achievement is understood and communicated within an Islamic framework at school level, not just by individual teachers
Excellence is framed as amanah, effort as ibadah, failure as a test, in assemblies, communications, and teacher practice
Assessment conversations with students include the Islamic dimension of why they work and what success means for a Muslim
5: Students articulate their academic goals in Islamic terms, they experience achievement as a spiritual act, not just a performance
A student asked 'why does your grade matter?' gives an Islamic answer, not just an academic one
The Islamic framework for academic life has replaced rather than supplemented a secular one

3B4.name: Critical Thinking Through an Islamic Lens
3B4.focus: Are students equipped to evaluate knowledge, media, and culture from an Islamic epistemological foundation, or are they consumers of secular content with no Islamic filter?

1: Students not equipped to critically engage with secular knowledge or media from an Islamic perspective
Islamic critical thinking is not a feature of any subject, students have no Islamic evaluative framework
Students are more likely to adjust their Islam to fit what they learn than to evaluate what they learn through their Islam
2: Some Islamic critique of knowledge introduced in Islamic Studies only, a lesson on media, a session on Islamic critical thinking
Does not transfer to other subjects or to students' independent engagement with the world
Students switch off their Islamic lens when they leave Islamic Studies
3: Some teachers across subjects encourage students to evaluate content through an Islamic lens
Not systematic, depends on individual teachers and subjects
Students have some tools for Islamic critical thinking but do not apply them consistently or independently
4: Students are equipped with Islamic epistemological foundations that they apply across subjects and in their lives
In multiple observed or reported lessons, students are seen evaluating ideas, claims, or media through an Islamic lens
Students can articulate what makes something worth believing and worth questioning, from an Islamic standpoint
5: Students independently evaluate new information, media, and ideas through an Islamic epistemological lens, without being asked
When encountering a challenging idea (in science, social media, or peer culture), students evaluate it against their Islamic framework
Students can explain what Islamic epistemology means and why it matters, it is part of their intellectual identity

3B5.name: Connection Between Subjects and Iman
3B5.focus: Do students experience their academic learning as part of a seamless Islamic worldview, or do they live a divided intellectual life where school subjects and Islam have nothing to say to each other?

1: Students experience academic and Islamic education as entirely separate, Islam is what happens in Islamic Studies, academic learning is what happens everywhere else
The two do not speak to each other; students see no connection between their Maths lesson and their iman
This intellectual separation is the normal experience of the school, felt by both students and teachers
2: Students occasionally notice connections, a science topic about creation, a history topic about Muslim civilisation, but these are accidental, not planned
Students describe these moments as surprising rather than expected, they stand out because they are rare
Students are not equipped with a framework for making the connections themselves
3: Connections between subjects and iman are intentional in some classrooms and some subjects
Students have some experience of integrated learning but not consistently enough to reshape how they see their education overall
Most students still experience their academic and Islamic lives as primarily separate
4: Students consistently experience their academic learning as part of a seamless Islamic worldview, all knowledge belongs to Allah is an experience, not just a phrase
In multiple conversations with students, they describe how their subjects connect to their iman without being prompted
The school's academic culture reinforces rather than competes with Islamic formation
5: Students articulate a coherent Islamic epistemology, they have a framework for why all knowledge is connected and how learning strengthens iman
Academic learning and Islamic formation are inseparable in how students understand their education
Students describe their iman as genuinely strengthened by academic learning, not despite it

3C.name: Prophetic Pedagogy in Teaching Approach
3C.description: Does the curriculum reflect how the Prophet (SAW) actually taught, or is it lecture-delivery with Islamic content?

3C1.name: Personalised and Contextual Teaching
3C1.focus: Do teachers know each student's Islamic formation needs, and adapt how they teach accordingly, as the Prophet (SAW) gave different guidance based on who was asking?

1: Teaching is entirely uniform, same delivery for every student regardless of Islamic formation background or stage
No teacher in the school adapts their tarbiyyah approach based on knowledge of individual students
Differentiation, if it exists, is purely academic, formation needs never considered
2: Some differentiation for academic ability exists but formation needs are not considered
Teachers might give a struggling student extra help with content but would not adapt their spiritual approach based on knowing that student personally
Formation differentiation does not exist as a concept in this school
3: Some teachers know their students' Islamic formation needs and make informal adjustments
This is personal instinct, not systematic, a naturally gifted murabbi adapts; others do not
No school-wide expectation or structure for personalised formation teaching
4: Teachers understand each student's Islamic formation needs and adapt delivery accordingly
At least in Islamic Studies and Adab, teachers can describe specific students' formation journeys and how their approach adapts
Personalised formation teaching is a valued practice, not just an individual teacher's gift
5: Students describe feeling individually known and guided in their deen, not as a class but as a person
Teaching adapts to students' spiritual journeys, not just their grades or academic level
A student who is struggling with doubt receives different pastoral and formative attention than one who is thriving in iman, and both experience this as care, not labelling

3C2.name: Questions Before Answers
3C2.focus: Does the teaching culture use questions to draw out student wisdom and invite reflection, as the Prophet (SAW) consistently did, or is teaching primarily information delivery?

1: Teaching across the observed curriculum is almost entirely information delivery, teachers provide answers, students receive them
Questions used to check comprehension only: recall, definition, procedure
No higher-order or identity-based inquiry observed in any lesson
2: Questions asked to check understanding, but none invite reflection on how this connects to who students are
Questions are closed: there is a right answer the teacher is looking for
Questions that diverge from expected answers are redirected without exploration
3: Some teachers use genuine open questions that invite student reflection
Quality and frequency are inconsistent, these questions appear in some lessons, not others
Students are not consistently trained or encouraged to ask their own deeper questions
4: Teachers use thoughtful questions throughout lessons to draw out student thinking and develop wisdom, not just to test knowledge
Questions invite students to connect knowledge to their identity as Muslims and to make judgements, not just recall facts
Students feel safe enough to ask their own questions, the culture of inquiry is two-directional
5: Students ask deep, unprompted questions, the classroom is a space of genuine inquiry modelled on Prophetic pedagogy
Questions from students reveal real internal engagement: they are processing, wrestling, and growing, not performing
The questioning culture extends beyond Islamic Studies, students bring Islamic questions to general subjects and vice versa

3C3.name: Stories and Narrative as Formation
3C3.focus: Is the Seerah and Islamic narrative a central formation tool in the curriculum, or is it mentioned briefly and moved on from?

1: Prophetic stories and Quranic narratives are not used as teaching tools in the observed curriculum
Seerah is limited to a historical timeline in Islamic Studies with no formation intent
The emotional and moral power of Islamic narrative is entirely absent from how the curriculum is taught
2: Stories used occasionally but as illustration only, supporting a factual point rather than forming the heart of a lesson
The emotional depth of the Seerah is not drawn on, stories are told, not experienced
Students receive Seerah passively; it does not appear to shape their moral imagination
3: Seerah and Islamic stories used as a regular teaching tool in Islamic Studies
Delivery varies, some teachers bring stories alive with skill; others recount them historically
Narrative is not yet a consistent formation tool across the broader curriculum
4: Islamic narrative is a central formation tool, teachers use stories of the Prophets and Sahabah with skill and emotional depth
Stories are not just told but explored, students asked to sit with them, connect them to their lives, and learn from them
The Seerah functions as a living reference across subjects, not just as history in Islamic Studies
5: Students reference Prophetic stories in their own speech and decision-making, narrative has shaped their moral imagination
When asked how they thought about a difficult decision, students invoke a Prophetic example naturally
Students can tell the story themselves, they are carriers of Islamic narrative, not just recipients of it

3C4.name: Experiential and Reflective Learning
3C4.focus: Is the curriculum designed to create formation experiences, not just transmit formation information?

1: Lessons across the curriculum are entirely theoretical, content explained and tested
No experiential or reflective dimension exists in any lesson observed
Students could complete the Islamic curriculum from memory without it affecting their inner life
2: Brief reflective moments exist in some lessons but are not designed or built into curriculum structure
Teachers invite reflection occasionally, 'what do you take from this?', without time, structure, or expectation that it produces anything
Experiential learning does not exist in the Islamic curriculum
3: Some experiential activities exist within the Islamic curriculum, group reflections, case studies, occasional structured activities
Quality and regularity are inconsistent
Muhasabah as a practice is introduced in some contexts but is not a systematic curriculum feature
4: The curriculum regularly includes experience, reflection, and discussion that develops students' Islamic identity, not just their Islamic knowledge
Reflective components are designed into lesson plans, not improvised
Muhasabah, self-examination, is introduced, practiced, and expected across Islamic Studies and Adab
5: Students seek out reflective spaces, they have developed the habit of muhasabah as a formation practice
When asked about their Islamic development, students describe their internal journey, not just what they have learned but how they have changed
Reflective learning has produced students who are self-aware, honest about their struggles, and growing, formation is visible, not just claimed

3C5.name: Teacher as Murabbi in Curriculum Delivery
3C5.focus: Is the teacher's role as murabbi embedded in how the curriculum is designed, or is it entirely dependent on individual teacher personality?

1: Curriculum design contains no murabbi dimension, lesson plans are content-focused with no pastoral hooks, formation goals, or reflective components
Teachers are content deliverers, the curriculum does not expect or support anything else
Formation, if it happens, is entirely accidental, the result of individual teacher character, not curriculum design
2: Some teachers take a murabbi approach but this is personal preference, not curriculum expectation
Lesson plans do not include formation goals, any formative element is added by the teacher on top of the curriculum, not built in
A teacher who leaves takes their murabbi approach with them; the curriculum does not retain it
3: The school articulates the expectation that teachers are murabbis in their delivery
Some curriculum documents begin to reflect this, schemes of work occasionally include a pastoral or formation note
In practice, the murabbi dimension is mostly personal to the teacher, not embedded in curriculum design
4: Curriculum designed with the teacher-as-murabbi role embedded, lesson plans include pastoral hooks, formation goals, and reflective components as standard
Teachers across Islamic subjects plan with formation intent, the curriculum expects it, not just the teacher's personality
A new teacher taking over a scheme of work would be guided toward a murabbi approach, not just content delivery
5: Students experience every lesson as an opportunity for formation, not just instruction
Students name teachers as mentors, not just educators, and connect specific curriculum moments to their personal growth
The murabbi dimension is self-sustaining: when individual teachers change, the formation intent remains because it is built into the design

3D.name: Student Outcomes & Minimum Nisab
3D.description: Is there a clear definition of what a graduate of this school should know and be, and is the curriculum producing it?

3D1.name: Definition of the Graduate Profile
3D1.focus: Is there a clear, agreed profile of what a graduate of this school should know and be, and does the curriculum consciously work toward it?

1: No defined Islamic graduate profile exists
Leadership and staff have vague, personal ideas about what a Year 12 graduate should be like, never articulated, agreed, or written down
The curriculum is not built toward any articulated formation outcome
2: Some informal expectations exist, 'we want students who pray regularly', 'we want students confident in their identity', but never written, agreed, or operationalised
Different staff have different ideas; no shared picture has been built
The curriculum reflects the graduate profile aspirationally but does not use it as a design tool
3: Some informal, shared understanding of graduate expectations exists, leadership and Islamic Studies teachers broadly agree on what they're aiming for
Not written or formally adopted as a curriculum target
The curriculum is not consciously designed toward a graduate profile but aligns with it loosely
4: A clear, agreed Islamic graduate profile exists, covering knowledge, practice, character, and identity
The profile is written and known; curriculum is consciously designed to build toward it
A teacher reviewing their Year 7 scheme of work can explain how it contributes to the Year 12 graduate profile
5: Students can articulate the graduate profile in their own words and see themselves working toward it
The graduate profile functions as a shared vision across the whole school, staff, students, and parents can describe it
The curriculum is reviewed and adjusted based on whether it is producing the graduate profile, it is a living tool, not a document

3D2.name: Minimum Nisab Framework
3D2.focus: Is there a defined minimum threshold of Islamic knowledge and practice that every student should meet, and is the curriculum systematically building toward it?

1: No minimum nisab or threshold has been defined
No one in the school can answer: 'What should every Year 12 graduate know, practise, and be able to do Islamically, at a minimum?'
The curriculum has no floor: students may graduate with serious Islamic formation gaps and the school would not know
2: The idea of a minimum nisab has been discussed
Some elements exist informally (Quran memorisation targets, salah expectations) but are not coherent or adopted as a school-wide framework
No systematic check of whether students are meeting a minimum formation threshold
3: A draft minimum nisab exists in some form, leadership has articulated key components
Not formally adopted, not embedded in the curriculum, and not used to assess or support individual students
The school knows what the framework should look like but has not yet built it
4: A Minimum Nisab framework is in place, covering salah, Quran, du'a, hadith, and character, and used to assess and support every student
The framework is known by staff and students; students know what the minimum expectations are
When a student is not meeting the minimum nisab, the school has a pastoral and curriculum response
5: Students know the Minimum Nisab and take ownership of meeting it, it functions as a personal aspiration, not just a school measure
Students can describe their own minimum nisab journey, what they have achieved, what they are working toward
The framework is used generatively, students and teachers use it to plan, celebrate, and pursue formation

3D3.name: Tracking Islamic Formation
3D3.focus: Does the school have any systematic way of tracking student Islamic formation, or is formation entirely invisible to the data systems that drive school decisions?

1: No tracking of student formation whatsoever, academic attainment is the only data the school holds on students
Formation is entirely invisible to any review, reporting, or intervention system
A student who is academically strong but spiritually struggling would not be identifiable from any school data
2: Some informal pastoral notes about individual students exist, a year head's awareness, a teacher's concern
This is personal knowledge, not systemic, it lives in people, not shared records
When that person moves on, the formation knowledge of that student is lost
3: Some pastoral recording of student formation exists, concern flags, pastoral meeting notes
Not systematic: only students with visible problems are tracked; quietly struggling students are invisible
Formation tracking is reactive and deficit-focused, it records problems, not journeys
4: A systematic, light-touch approach to tracking student formation exists alongside academic tracking
Tracking is qualitative, dignified, and growth-focused, not a surveillance tool but a pastoral and formation guide
All students have some formation record, not just those with problems, and it is used in pastoral conversations
5: Students reflect on their own formation journey, tracking is a tool for self-awareness, not just staff reporting
Formation data is used in school-wide review conversations: 'How are students doing in their Islamic identity?' has an evidence-based answer
The tracking system has produced insights that shaped curriculum or pastoral decisions, it is used, not just maintained

3D4.name: Student Identity and Sense of Belonging
3D4.focus: Do graduates leave this school with a secure, positive Muslim identity, or with an identity that is fragile, defensive, or confused?

1: Students appear uncertain about or disconnected from their Muslim identity
Identity confusion is visible, students who distance themselves from Islamic identity, or who perform it without owning it
The school has not resolved the dual-identity challenge: students feel torn between being Muslim and being Australian
2: Some students show a positive Muslim identity but outcomes are highly inconsistent
Identity strength appears to depend on family background rather than school formation, the school is not systematically producing it
Students who struggle with identity are not identified or supported
3: Positive Muslim identity is a visible outcome for many students but inconsistent across year groups
Some year groups have strong collective Islamic identity; others do not
The school forms identity in some students but has not yet found a way to do it reliably for all
4: Graduates leave with a secure, positive Muslim identity, proud of their deen, equipped to live it, and connected to a community of believers
This is a visible pattern, not an occasional outcome, most students across year groups demonstrate a settled sense of who they are as Muslims
The school has successfully navigated the dual-identity challenge: students are confidently Muslim and confidently Australian
5: Students describe their Muslim identity as a source of strength and pride, it shapes their goals, relationships, and sense of self
Muslim identity is not defended or performed, it is owned and expressed naturally
Alumni describe their time at this school as formative for who they are as Muslims, not just what they know

3D5.name: Student Engagement and Love of Deen
3D5.focus: Do students show genuine love for and curiosity about their deen, or are they compliant with Islamic expectations they do not own?

1: Students are disengaged from or openly resistant to Islamic learning
Islamic education is experienced as imposed, something done to students, not with them
Students switch off visibly in Islamic Studies and show no Islamic engagement outside of structured time
2: Students comply with Islamic expectations, attend salah, sit in Islamic Studies, but show no genuine enthusiasm or personal connection to faith
The compliance is school-driven, not internally motivated
Students who leave the school may drop Islamic practices quickly, they were performing for the school, not for Allah
3: Students are generally cooperative with Islamic education and some show genuine interest
Love of deen is present in individuals but it is not a school-wide culture
Many students experience Islamic education as school work, not personal growth
4: Students across the school show genuine love for and curiosity about their deen, not performance for the teacher but authentic relationship with Allah
Students initiate Islamic conversations, ask questions beyond what the syllabus requires, and describe their faith as something they choose
Islamic engagement is visible outside structured time: in how students talk, what they reference, how they navigate challenges
5: Students initiate Islamic conversations, activities, and questions beyond the timetable, love of deen is a visible, school-wide culture
A student from this school is identifiably different in their relationship with Islam compared to a student from a school where Islam is merely taught
Alumni describe their love of deen as something the school cultivated, it is one of the school's visible formation outcomes
